introduction:
Something was missing from the earth for a very long time.

After Jesus Christ established His Church and His apostles were killed, the authority to lead the Church was lost. Doctrines were changed. Ordinances were altered. For over a thousand years, the fulness of the gospel was not on the earth. But God had a plan to bring it back.

This book tells the story of how the gospel was restored. You'll learn about the Great Apostasy -- what was lost and why it mattered. You'll see how God prepared the world over centuries so that the Restoration could happen. You'll follow Joseph Smith from a farm boy with a question to the prophet who received more revelation than any other person in modern history. You'll walk with him through the First Vision, the coming forth of the Book of Mormon, the restoration of the priesthood, the building of temples, and the organization of the Church.

You'll also see the hardships -- the persecution, the mobs, the betrayals, Liberty Jail, and the martyrdom at Carthage. And you'll see the Church survive it all, with Brigham Young leading the Saints forward.

This is your story. The Restoration didn't just happen to people in the 1800s -- it's the reason your church, your priesthood, your temples, and your covenants exist today. Understanding how it all began helps you understand what it all means.

Each lesson takes about 15-20 minutes. Read the lesson, talk it over with the AI tutor, then answer a few questions in your own words. There's no rush -- the goal is real understanding, not speed.
description: A continuous narrative of the Restoration of the gospel of Jesus Christ -- from the Great Apostasy through Joseph Smith's martyrdom and Brigham Young becoming president. Written for Latter-day Saint kids, covering the First Vision, the Book of Mormon, the restoration of the priesthood, the building of temples, and the early history of the Church.
what_youll_learn: What was lost during the Great Apostasy and why restoration was necessary
How God prepared the world for the Restoration over centuries
Joseph Smith's early life and the question that changed the world
What happened in the Sacred Grove during the First Vision
How the Book of Mormon came forth and was translated
How the Aaronic and Melchizedek Priesthoods were restored
How the Church was organized and began to grow
What happened in Kirtland, Missouri, and Nauvoo
The martyrdom of Joseph and Hyrum Smith and the succession to Brigham Young
expectations: 15 chapters, designed for one chapter per week (4 short lessons per week). Each lesson takes about 15-20 minutes -- read the lesson, talk it over with the AI tutor, then answer a few short questions in your own words. Your work is graded by the tutor and you can take a chapter as many times as you need.
prerequisites: None. No prior Church history knowledge is assumed. Pairs well with History of Israel for Kids and The Ministry of Jesus Christ for Kids but does not depend on them.
tutor_instructions:
This book covers the Restoration of the gospel from the Great Apostasy through Brigham Young becoming president, written for Latter-day Saint kids ages 7-12. The reader has likely heard many of these stories in Primary and Come Follow Me but may not have a clear sense of the full timeline.

When explaining and when grading:
- Use plain, simple words. Prefer short sentences. Avoid academic or theological jargon.
- LDS perspective is the default. Joseph Smith was a prophet. The First Vision happened. The Book of Mormon is true. The priesthood was restored. These are not debated.
- Be matter-of-fact, not apologetic. These kids are already members.
- Help kids connect the Restoration timeline to their lives today: 'The priesthood that was restored by John the Baptist is the same priesthood that was used at your baptism.'
- When discussing difficult events (persecution, Haun's Mill, the martyrdom), be honest but age-appropriate. Don't sanitize, but don't dwell on graphic details.
- Be reverent when discussing sacred events (the First Vision, temple dedications, the martyrdom) but not stiff.
- When grading, accept any answer that captures the lesson's core idea in the student's own words. Don't require exact dates unless the question specifically asks.
- Be encouraging. The goal is to help kids see the Restoration as their own story.
